ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for mapo-style ramen soup; closest parent is ramen noodle soup (FOODON_00002700).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent; no specific code for mapo-style ramen soup exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Ken Mapo Soup Special as served at Ramen Matsuri, approximately 500 g, comprising ramen noodles, mapo tofu sauce, silken tofu, ground pork, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Mapo tofu ramen soup — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: ramen noodles (SR Legacy #20124), mapo tofu (estimated from tofu and ground pork components), and pork broth
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Ken Mapo Soup Special or mapo ramen soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked ramen noodles, silken tofu, ground pork (cooked), pork/chicken broth, and standard mapo sauce ingredients (doubanjiang, sesame oil, soy sauce). Estimated composition per 100 g of finished soup: approximately 60% broth, 15% noodles, 12% tofu, 8% ground pork, 5% sauce/seasonings. Values are moderate-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: Noodle soups and ramen GI estimation — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles in broth (GI ~52, Atkinson et al. 2008, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ken Mapo Soup Special or mapo ramen soup. GI of 52 is estimated from cooked wheat noodles in broth as the closest analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The mapo tofu sauce components (tofu, pork, doubanjiang) contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 47.5) / 100 = 24.7, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 47.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth, soy sauce, and doubanjiang
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Ken Mapo Soup Special due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 2600 mg per 500 g serving), which represents over 100% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients; requesting reduced-sodium broth and sauce is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat ramen noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that Ken Mapo Soup Special has a moderate glycemic load of approximately 24.7 per bowl (500 g) driven by the wheat ramen noodles (GI ~52); portion control and pairing with the protein-rich mapo tofu component can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from ground pork and sesame oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Ken Mapo Soup Special with caution due to its high sodium content (~2600 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~6.5 g per serving) from ground pork and cooking oils, both of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; reduced-sodium and leaner protein modifications are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ken Mapo Soup Special is a Japanese-style ramen dish served at Ramen Matsuri in Singapore, combining the bold, numbing-spicy flavours of Chinese mapo tofu with a Japanese ramen noodle soup format. The dish features wheat ramen noodles submerged in a rich, spicy broth made from chicken or pork stock infused with doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), Sichuan peppercorn, garlic, ginger, and chilli oil. Silken tofu and seasoned ground pork are key components, contributing protein and a creamy, soft texture that contrasts with the chewy noodles. The soup is finished with sesame oil and scallions, delivering a complex umami-forward, fiery flavour profile with a characteristic numbing sensation from Sichuan peppercorn. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 98 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 4.2 g fat, and 9.5 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g due to the broth and fermented condiments. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low-medium boundary) based on the wheat noodle component. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the noodle-driven glycemic load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to combined sodium and saturated fat content. It is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, or halal diets in its standard form. This dish exemplifies the Japanese culinary tradition of adapting Chinese flavour profiles into ramen formats, a genre known as 'fusion ramen' popular in Singapore's Japanese restaurant scene.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Ken Mapo Soup Special or mapo ramen soup exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for constituent ingredients. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked wheat noodles in broth) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no direct analytical measurement of this specific dish exists in any approved database. 2. Sodium content is a key clinical concern and may vary significantly between restaurant preparations; the estimate of 520 mg/100 g is based on typical ramen broth sodium levels and should be treated as an approximation. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported or estimable from the available USDA component data for this dish type. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the constituent ingredients under standard preparation. 5. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles in broth per Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for mapo ramen soup exists. 6. The dish is served at Ramen Matsuri, 7 North Canal Road, Singapore, as listed on field survey data at SGD 22.70. 7. Halal status is unconfirmed; the dish contains pork and is therefore not halal in its standard form. 8. The food_id GD002607 is preserved from the input seed record.
